task: I need a practical way to audit a batch of client files before they go out, so I don’t miss anything obvious and end up cleaning up a mistake later. Can you help me set up a quick review process and a short checklist I can actually use?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it simple and repeatable.

## Quick review process
1. **Check the basics first**
   - Correct client name
   - Correct account/file number
   - Right date/version
   - Right package or document set

2. **Verify the key facts**
   - Amounts match
   - Dates are current
   - Signatures/approvals are present
   - Any required attachments are included

3. **Look for obvious mismatches**
   - Wrong file in the batch
   - Missing pages
   - Conflicting numbers
   - Outdated forms or notes

4. **Do a final send check**
   - Is it going to the right person?
   - Is anything still missing?
   - Would this make sense to someone seeing it fresh?

## Short checklist
- [ ] Client name correct
- [ ] File/account number correct
- [ ] Current date/version
- [ ] All required docs included
- [ ] Amounts and figures match
- [ ] Signatures/approvals complete
- [ ] No missing pages
- [ ] No outdated forms
- [ ] Sent to the right recipient

## Best way to use it
- Review the whole batch once for structure
- Then spot-check each file against the checklist
- If anything looks off, stop and fix it before sending

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page reviewer checklist you can paste into a work note or email template.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right lane, but it’s still a little generic. I need something tighter for a banking file review — not just “missing pages” and “wrong recipient.”

Build it around the stuff that actually blows up later:
- client name / CIF or account number
- product type matches the file
- amount, rate, term, payment all line up
- signatures, initials, and dates are complete
- required disclosures / approvals are there
- ID docs and income docs if applicable
- exceptions or notes are documented
- no stale version of the form
- package is in the right order before it goes out

And I want the process in a simple order I can hand to a staff member:
1. identity
2. numbers
3. docs
4. signatures/approvals
5. compliance items
6. final package check

If you can, make it a one-page checklist with checkboxes and a “stop and fix” line at the bottom. That’s the kind of thing I can actually use without babysitting it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, banking-specific version you can hand off.

# File Review Checklist

## Quick review process
Review in this order:

### 1) Identity
- [ ] Client name matches throughout the file
- [ ] CIF / account number is correct
- [ ] Product type matches the file and request
- [ ] Borrower / applicant details are consistent

### 2) Numbers
- [ ] Amount matches across all documents
- [ ] Rate matches across all documents
- [ ] Term matches across all documents
- [ ] Payment amount matches across all documents

### 3) Documents
- [ ] Required forms are included
- [ ] ID documents are included if applicable
- [ ] Income documents are included if applicable
- [ ] No missing pages or missing sections
- [ ] No stale or outdated version of any form

### 4) Signatures / approvals
- [ ] All required signatures are present
- [ ] Initials are complete where needed
- [ ] Dates are complete and current
- [ ] Required approvals are in the file

### 5) Compliance items
- [ ] Required disclosures are included
- [ ] Any exceptions are documented
- [ ] Notes explain anything unusual
- [ ] No unresolved compliance gaps

### 6) Final package check
- [ ] Package is in the right order
- [ ] File is complete before it goes out
- [ ] Nothing obvious is left unresolved

## Stop and fix
**If anything is missing, mismatched, outdated, or unclear, stop and fix it before the file goes out. Do not send a file that still needs cleanup.**

If you want, I can also turn this into a shorter “staff version” that fits on one screen.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that works. Tight enough, and it’s usable without me having to rewrite half of it. The order makes sense, and the stop-and-fix line is exactly what I wanted. I can hand that to staff as-is.
